faqs.q: What is the difference between a differential diagnosis and a diagnosis?
faqs.a: The differential is the working list of possible conditions a clinician is weighing. The diagnosis is the conclusion they reach after gathering history, timing, severity, and other evidence to rule conditions in or out.

The right diagnosis points to the right treatment. Treating bipolar depression as if it were ordinary depression, for example, can make things worse, so sorting out the differential carefully matters.

faqs.q: Can a diagnosis change over time?
faqs.a: Yes. A differential isn't permanent. As new information appears or symptoms change, a clinician may revisit the list and revise the diagnosis.
